desc:
Rows of sunflower plants cover almost a mile of this area. When in bloom, the
bright orange and gold flowers are almost the twice the length of a hobbit's
foot. They provide shade for the small animals that graze on their stalks. Some
small stones dot the ground but, for the most part the soil is soft and rich in
nutrients. If this basin was not kept secret by the mountainous surroundings,
some farmer would have surely claimed it for crops.
name: Shallow Basin

desc:
This is the northernmost of a trio of mountains reaching far in to the sky. From
here looking directly south or at an angle to the southeast one can see straight
across to the other two, however, the clouds obscure the very top of these
triple peaks. The air is thin yet moist from the cloudy fog that seems to cling
to the peaks.
name: Cloudcapt Mountains

desc:
As sharp as the cuts in these cliffs are, it is surprising they are home to so
much greenery. The blue-green grasses give these cliffs an almost royal cloak.
Some large rocks could be mistaken for thrones on which one could sit or stand
tall shouting hollow proclamations into the valley below to the east.
name: Regal Cliffs

desc:
Nestled in between two large groups of mountains, this plateau is far above
sea-level. Bordered to the south and east by mountainside and to the west by
cliffs, this area appears to be safe from anything except that which would drop
from the sky. The low vine-like grass from the spread from the north covers the
ground. It grows a bit higher here and often catches the footsteps of travellers
only long enough for the thin roots to snap.
name: Highland Cove

desc:
Tall fir trees cover the top of this mountain. Their prickly evergreen leaves
cut the strong northern winds that often blow soil and stray branches or
sometimes even small trees to the south. Sparser than those trees to the north,
a traveller through these parts could look and see the sky.
name: Majestic Mountaintop

desc:
Trees almost thick enough to be a forest grow here in natural lines. They
stretch up tall into the sky adding to the mountain's height. Evergreen, they
have long trunks with few branches. The branches that do grow get shorter the
farther up the tree they grow. The ground beneath must have soil cover enough to
house the roots of these trees, as none poke out through the ground.
name: Majestic Mountaintop

desc:
The tall evergreen trees obscure the view of the surrounding mountains. If not
for the thinner air and the occasional fog from a low lying cloud one might
think this place is a lowland forest rather than a high mountain peak. The sound
of water rushing over rock can be heard coming from the north.
name: Majestic Mountaintop

desc:
The flow of the shallow water is so constant that it almost looks calm. A
falling leaf into the stream quickly shows otherwise. The current is quick and
its facade of serenity most deceiving. Travellers should take care not to let
their valuables fall into the stream lest they be swept away.
name: Rushing Rivulet

desc:
Rocks of various sizes piled one on top of another make up the ground. High
winds blowing down from the towering peaks to the west, over time, have sent
tons of rock, dirt and whole cliffs crashing down to these lower bluffs. Small
animals have tunnelled through the spaces between these materials. They build
homes that are often destroyed as quickly as it takes another rock to fall.
name: Arduous Mountain Bluffs

desc:
Clumps of dirt cling to rocks embedded in the ground. Small animals are able to
weave between the narrow gaps between the stones. Tufts of fur and the
occasional mark of dried blood and torn skin serve as a reminder that travel
here can be perilous if not taken slowly. A few scattered shrubs grow in the
shaded shelter of some of the larger rocks. Some flowering weeds give the ground
a touch of a colour other than brown dirt and the grey of the stone.
name: Rugged Headlands

desc:
The ground here appears somewhat level, forming a small eroding plateau which
crumbles at the southeast edge. From this low rocky rise one can look up at the
higher peaks to the northwest or down onto a rushing stream of water to the
southeast. Some wheat grasses have taken seed and grown close to a foot tall.
This secluded plateau seems to be a haven compared to the otherwise rocky and
treacherous terrain.
name: Eroding Mountain Bluffs

desc:
The ground slopes upward and eastward. The air thins the higher one travels.
There are some scattered weeds growing in the shelter of some large rocks,
however very little grass survives in this rocky terrain. Dirt and shards of
rock crumble under the footsteps of travellers.
name: Rugged Headlands

desc:
Shade trees grow here in the shadow of the western mountain range. Tall elm
trees with their large branches rise above smaller red maple and weeping willow
trees. Among the branches are the nests of birds, some in use, others left
abandoned for seasons. A soft thin layer of grass covers the ground.
name: Arduous Mountain Bluffs

desc:
Rushing out of a narrow gully to the west, Nimrodel now enters a wider and less
rocky channel. The line of cliffs which has marked the southern bounds of the
watercourse are now descending rapidly, and a second stream can be seen in the
distance, flowing toward a confluence with this brook. To the north the valley
is quite open, and the immediate area offers a gently sloping, grassy shoreline.
name: Nimrodel

desc:
Sloping down gently towards a stream, which sweeps here from the west to the
north, the land is beginning to gain a fertility which is absent further up the
valley. Though the topsoil is thin, it is well-watered, and a variety of tiny
wildflowers nestle among a thin carpet of grass. Rocks and stones have tumbled
here and there, but there are fewer here than there are closer to the cliffs and
ridges of the region.
name: In a Valley along a Stream

desc:
The land climbs steadily to the west, as it advances toward the mountains.
Immediately to the north a long ridgeline rises above you, a steep cliff face
offering the most direct route from here to its spine. The land here bears
blemishes from these slopes, being littered with sharp stones, the debris of
many past rockslides.
name: Below the Ridgeline

desc:
Jutting out into the path of the stream to the south, the valley floor here is
comprised of a broad table of solid granite. Tilted like the rest of the
surrounding land, it too slopes upward to the west and the north, following the
general contour of the vale in which it stands. The vast flat plate of stone,
with only the sparest covering of topsoil means that very few plants manage to
grow here.
name: In a Valley along a Stream

desc:
Flowing through a narrow gully, defined by the cliffs to the south, and a large,
protruding table of rock, which juts into the channel from the north, Nimrodel
is deep and very fast-flowing here. The presence of many large, sharp-sided
boulders beneath the rushing waters makes negotiating the area quite
troublesome. Many others break the surface, leading to a profusion of foamy
white caps.
name: The Narrows

desc:
A canopy of high branches covers this portion of the trail. While the trees are
younger than others of this region it would still take three elves, standing
with their feet on each other's shoulders, to touch the mossy green branches or
deep green leaves. During the day, the rays of sun, managing to seep through the
veil of green, create emerald streams of light that illuminate the trail.
name: A Trail through the Jade Trees

desc:
Through years of use and the constant pounding of footsteps, the hoofs of horses
and the erosion of time, this trail has been carved into the landscape. So
beaten down is the soil that it has solidified into a thick unmoldable clay.
Bordering the roadway, large trees stretch up towards the heavens.
name: Etched Trail

desc:
The ground of the trail lies lower than the forested land on either side as if
the trail was carved out by some hand from the heavens. The long jagged
scratches that run its length are deep and the ground is so hard it would take
insurmountable weight to flatten them back out.
name: Etched Trail

desc:
Not a single blade of grass pokes through the hard soil that makes up this
trail. The dark brown soil is more like molded clay than dirt. It has the
consistency of mud that has been petrified. With a sharp sword one might be able
to scrape away at the trail. From the looks of it, however, the sword might be
dented in the process. On the eastern side of the trail, a grove of ancient
knotted trees yawn eerily overhead.
name: Etched Trail

desc:
Set about a foot lower than the surrounding land this trail seems to have been
carved out long before the trees that grow along its sides were even saplings.
Now the tall tree roots are all that burst through the trail's hard clay. The
trail carries on to the north and to the west.
name: Etched Trail

desc:
A stone mason carving a block of granite might have tools that could carve a
deep trail such as this. A foot into the surrounding land this trail is made of
hard clay. Long ago the ground must have been softer and more easily molded as
deep scars have been cut into the length of the trail. The ground is now hard
and unyielding.
name: Etched Trail

desc:
Towering cliffs surround the trail here to the east and the west. Try as one
might it would be impossible to scale them and reach the lands above. The
crumbled rock and soil eroded from the cliffs above litter the ground here
giving it a speckled appearance. Some larger pieces of cliff have also fallen
and are wedged deep into the ground.
name: Narrow Mountain Trail

desc:
Much narrower than the trail to the south, this part of the trail is tucked
between tall cliffs to the east and west. Rocks, having fallen from above,
litter the trail. While many things have been known to fall from the lands
above, reaching those lands above from this part of the trail would be
impossible even for the greatest climber.
name: Narrow Mountain Trail

desc:
This old trail seems to have been carved out of the ground. It sits a few feet
into the surrounding land. It is plausible to think that this hard clay lies
below all the surrounding pastures. This might explain the lack of vegetation
that would grow deeper than a few inches into the soil. To the north, the trail
narrows as it leads into the mountains.
name: Etched Trail

desc:
This far stretching trail seems to lead in one direction toward the mountains to
the north. In the other, it winds through the hills and meadowlands. Etched into
the ground at least two feet not a stray seed can break the surface to take
root. The hard clay trail is almost impossible to mar.
name: Etched Trail

desc:
The wild grasses in the meadow to the south barely encroach upon this deeply cut
trail. Strewn seedlings that are blown on the wind merely sit upon the trail's
hard surface unable to penetrate what once was dirt but over centuries has
hardened to become clay the density of rock.
name: Etched Trail

desc:
This hardened trail is much rougher on the hoofs of horses than the surrounding
lands. The lack of vegetation however makes it more easily passable than cutting
through the meadowlands. So hard is the ground it may be mistaken for rock.
Continuing east and west it is clearly well used and fit for travelling.
name: Etched Trail

desc:
From here the trail continues miles to the east and just as far southward. It
appears to be carved a few feet into the ground. Deep scratches indicate a great
force etched it into the ground, removing the fertile soil and leaving this
hard, clay layer exposed.
name: Etched Trail

desc:
The ground of the trail is a little more malleable in these parts than it is
farther to the east. Depressions in the thick clay sometimes reveal hoof prints
and the heel prints of some travellers on foot. Those choosing to tread lightly
could easily avoid leaving any impressions. The trail appears to end here
however, fading into the western prairie.
name: Etched Trail

desc:
The tall wheat grass, that grows undisturbed elsewhere, seems to have a fierce
enemy in those that travel here. Wanderers off a westerly trail have trampled
the grains and cleared large portions when hastily making camp. Ashes of
campfires litter the ground. These scattered barren spots where fires were lit
long ago are starting to show some little signs of being reclaimed by the sea of
grain.
name: Amber Prairie

desc:
Grains the colour of the burning sun stretch for miles. Their tall stems sway
with the wind as it blows. They bow and bend as even the smallest of insects
land upon their single stalks. The ground, three feet below their golden tips,
is littered with the remnants of the previous seasons' growth.
name: Amber Prairie

desc:
Scattered rocks and clumps of dirt lie in these mountain foothills. A few sturdy
plants have managed to grow in the uneven and ever-shifting terrain. Fresh
layers of rock and dirt that fall from the higher peaks, often strike dry dirt,
kicking it up into the air.
name: Rising Steeps

desc:
This thin ledge can be followed up and down the western side of the mountain. A
few hundred feet wide, it allows travellers an easier way to traverse the steep
mountainside. Rocks, clumps of dirt and vegetation have fallen from the upper
cliffs and lie here embedded deep into the ground.
name: Tapered Mountain Ridge

desc:
Jagged and sharp are the best words to describe the rocks jutting out from this
mountain slope. A fallen tree has impaled itself on one of the larger shards of
rock. One can imagine the force at which it must have fallen. Its trunk is now
split in two, with the sharp point of the rock still exposed.
name: Coarse Mountainside

desc:
This mountain is the perfect companion of its mate to the northeast. Both are
the perfect places to gaze at the work of Tintalle the Kindler, the creator of
the stars. Her husband Manwe, master of air and weather, must think this a good
place to honour her as well because the weather here is milder than in the
surrounding areas and rarely does a cloud block the view of his beloved's
artistry.
name: Celestial Summit

desc:
This shelf is so narrow that travellers must pass here in single file. Battle
here would be very treacherous, as there is no place to hide and only two ways
to run, and the choice between the high summit to the north or the dangerous
cliffs to the east would fill those in a panic with equal dismay. There are a
few crevices in the mountain wall that might provide one with a place to hide
but for the briefest period of time.
name: Slender Mountain Shelf

desc:
This flat parcel of land is nestled between a mountain summit and a steep drop
downward. The ground is dark brown dirt and in some places is flecked with bits
of grey materials that look like ashes. There are some sporadic patches of grass
and a few stray bushes.
name: Verdant Mesa

desc:
At the apex of this western mountain, one can look straight across to the lower
peaks in the east. On a clear day even a glimpse of the far off meadowlands can
be gleamed. The air here is thin and clean with a scent that can only be
described as resembling the air after a fresh spring rain.
name: Lofty Zenith

desc:
Nestled within the protection of the mountains, this fertile basin is home to a
plentiful variety of plants and animals. Shaded from the sun at all times but
noon the air here is cool and crisp. Because of its secluded location, this
basin has barely been touched. Had it been seen by many, surely some farmer
would wish to cultivate this place.
name: Sheltered Basin

desc:
For miles westward, the only way to travel is upward. The land makes a quick
shift from rolling hills to steep rising mountains that hinder travellers'
passage. Shards of rock, broken off the high cliffs, lie here, embedded deep in
the ground by the force of their fall.
name: Rising Steeps

desc:
A few small rocks are trapped in the clay that forms the trail. At some point
this hardened ground must have been soft enough for the rocks to penetrate the
surface. Now, not even the heaviest rider on the largest steed would make any
imprint in this hard surface.
name: Etched Trail

desc:
As if it was a carved moat, dug two feet deep in the ground, this sunken clay
trail serves to divide these fertile grasslands. Ridges of the hard clay line
either side of the trail here about ankle high. These ridges and the lack of
nutrients in the clay to nurture plant life keeps the vegetation from
encroaching upon the trail.
name: Etched Trail

desc:
When this volcano erupted, a pool of lava filled the inner cavern to the west.
It gathered there, pushing out in all directions. Some of the rock on the
western edge of this area gave way, sending lava crashing eastward and creating
this corridor. Since then, the weight of the mountain above has born down on
this point, causing much of the area to be filled with charred and broken rock.
name: Collapsed Corridor

desc:
A cloudy haze engulfs this summit. Oblivious to the changing of the seasons,
snowflakes lie upon the ground. Towards the centre of the summit a long jagged
ridge comes to halt in the form of a great crack in the ground. More than large
enough for a man to enter, this opening in the earth seems to delve down into
this mountain's secrets.
name: Mountain Summit

desc:
Only a little light passes through the entrance to this small crack in the
mountainside. The remnants of fires that were probably made by those who found
this quiet place to rest from their arduous climb, cover the ground. Several
sketches have been carved into the stone mountain wall.
name: Within the Mountain Crevice

desc:
Flowing through small cracks and fissures in the rock of the cliff faces,
numerous tiny rivulets gather here, to form the beginnings of a mountain stream.
The main channel, which flows in from the northwest, does so along a most narrow
and steeply sloped notch between the cliffs, which is quite impassable for any
person.
name: A Rocky Rill

desc:
A narrow strip of sloping land runs down from a high mesa towards the top of a
soaring cliff. As crisp and sharp as a knife edge, the margin of these highlands
stretches off to the west, and sweeps round to the northeast, but the terrain
quickly becomes broken and uneven, and undoubtedly quite unsafe for travel.
name: At the Top of a Precipice

desc:
A long, narrow plateau stretches out to the east, like a vast table-top. Far
above the floor of the valley below, it is an area of some seclusion, and the
higher mountain peaks and ridges hereabouts make it quite well hidden. To the
south the land falls off quite rapidly, as it approaches the top of a mighty
cliff.
name: Atop a Plateau

desc:
You find yourself upon a long, narrow plateau, which rises high above the
surrounding lands. Hidden as it is, at a high elevation, and sheltered from view
by so many mountain peaks and ridges, it is a comparitively quiet and
undisturbed area. As flat as a table-top, it stretches away to the west, and
near its centre a small body of water nourishes its soils.
name: Atop a Plateau

desc:
The strange route has broken off from the path to the north and runs north-east
here, winding among the rocks and the boulders. It is not marked in any way and
rather hard to follow, and it seems that any rockslide could make it disappear
altogether.
name: Off the Path

desc:
A small concealed path cuts through the mountain range, enshrouded by the high
rockfaces and completely hidden from view. A small rocky ledge sprouts to the
north. South lies an area which you really can't seem to make out. A trail
begins just to the east, and part of this path is to the west.
name: On a Hidden Mountain Path

desc:
A small rocky ledge juts out from the path, curving alongside the mountain and
providing a narrow walking space for the curious. The ledge continues to the
north, and you can also go back to the path which lies just to the south.
name: A Rock Ledge

desc:
The trail continues onwards stretching from east to west. There is not much to
see except the small stones and pebbles that line the floor. Your vision is
completely blocked by the rock walls that surround the trail. A crisp and fresh
scent is carried on the air by the mountain wind.
name: A Turn in the Trail

desc:
A natural fault line in the rock of the cliffs has been eroded and enlarged by
centuries of dripping water. The walls of the resulting tunnel meet at a point
quite far above your head, but even at floor level the passage is not wide, so
that anyone particularly tall would find their shoulders brushing against the
cold, hard stone. Whilst to the south the channel gets no wider, to the north it
begins to open out into a larger cavern.
name: In a Cramped Tunnel

desc:
Nestled between two commanding cliff-faces, which meet in an almost perfect vee
at the western end of the valley, a narrow mountain vale opens out before you.
Eastward it rolls down from the mountains, toward the lowlands of the Great
River and the mighty forest of Lothlorien. But here, amid the rocks and boulders
of this craggy land, only a little vegetation manages to survive.
name: At the Head of the Valley

desc:
Some sort of a natural cataclysm must have visited this section of the ridge,
for the sharp saddle has been reduced to a wider, flatter table, rimmed by
steep, crumbling walls. The ground is a little lower here than it is further
east along the ridge, and progress further westward, as well as climbing down
from the ridge either to the north or to the south, has been rendered quite
impossible by the highly unstable, crumbling walls of this small basin.
name: On a Blasted Ridge

desc:
Sheer drop-offs to either side make progress along this steep ridge rather
perilous. However, the elevation of the lands below you to the south is rather
greater than that of the valley lying north, and it might be possible to make
the southerly descent in safety. Westward the path becomes steeper, and the
ground appears to be more scarred by cracks and fissures.
name: A Crumbling Ridgeline

desc:
The valley becomes ever more narrow as it climbs west into the arms of the
mountains. Sheer walls, quite unclimable due to their weak, crumbling stone,
rear up before you to the south, whilst to the east the landscape opens out as
it loses altitude. The ground about you is littered with rocks and stones, which
stand out like an archipelago, amid a sea of thin grass.
name: A Climbing Vale

desc:
Steep walls sweep around you to the north and west, confining the valley. The
ground here is rather poor, with numerous rocks and boulders littering the
valley floor, but to the south and especially to the west, where the valley
opens out and loses altitude, the land seems to be at least a little richer and
less rocky.
name: A Narrowing Valley

desc:
Bordered to the north and south by the sprawling arms of two mountain ridges,
this narrow valley offers a refuge for all manner of wildlife. Wildflowers and a
few small shrubs grow on the sloping ground, and the short-cropped grasses
attest to the existence here of a variety of animals.
name: A Rising Valley

desc:
Narrow and sloping, confined to the north and south by craggy ridges, the valley
floor rises steadily to the west, where it climbs to the base of one of the
Misty Mountains. Though not the tallest peak by far, this lofty eminence
nevertheless casts long shadows across the entire vale for much of the day, and
sunset must come early to this land.
name: A Rising Valley

desc:
Crumbling and rocky, this narrow ridge makes its rather precarious way from east
to west, where it ascends into the mountains. The terrain has something of the
look of a path, but either it is one which has long since fallen into disuse, or
perhaps it is nothing more than a game trail. Whichever is true, the fact
remains that this is no longer an easy route to travel.
name: A Crumbling Ridgeline

desc:
Stretching from east to west, a long ridge runs like a scar across the land just
north of here. Southward the ground slopes gently down toward the banks of a
lively stream, whose waters enable even such poor and rocky terrain as this to
support a variety of vegetation. Although in this immediate area it is largely
restricted to grasses and wildflowers, further to the east some bushes and
shrubs begin to take hold, marking the very outskirts of a forest.
name: Between Ridge and Stream

desc:
Fed by the waters of a southern tributary, Nimrodel has gained in stature when
compared to her earlier reaches. But she remains a rather small and very shallow
waterway, which could easily be forded at most points. Here the stream travels
through an area of fairly craggy land, and this has made her channel quite
rocky, but the waters roll gently past the obstructions, since the current is
quite sedate.
name: A Burgeoning Mountain Stream

desc:
A major tributary flows into Nimrodel from the south here, their waters merging
into a lively, but still rather shallow stream. Sweeping from east to north, the
beck curves gracefully past the outskirts of a forest, which stand on slightly
higher ground, then the stream travels on northwestward, toward an eventual
confluence with the far mightier Celebrant.
name: At a Confluence of Two Streams

desc:
The fast, steady flow of the water cleanses away the dust and dirt from the
banks. Some such materials gather here. Travellers should be wary not to be
tripped up by the swift current as they cross into the trees to the east, gazing
into what treasures may have been carried to this shimmering rock bed.
name: Rushing Rivulet

desc:
Tranquil in appearance, these waters are anything but still. The quick current
flows amazingly steady hence the deception. The silver rocks that line the
riverbed do not move in the current. They merely stick firm into the clay below
and let the water wear them down. The stream stretches on to the north and west.
name: Rushing Rivulet

desc:
Water, as crystal clear as fine blown glass, flows here at a very constant pace.
At first glance the water looks still and calm, yet it truly does flow as quick
as blood through veins. Should it not flow so swiftly, leaves and dirt would
gather in such masses that this water would not be fit to drink.
name: Rushing Rivulet

desc:
Not fields of gold, nor forests of green, compare in brilliance to the silver
shine of this rivulet. In day, the sun highlights its wonder. At night, the
moon's light gives the rocky stream such a glow it almost lights the surrounding
area. Travellers might see a magical quality in this water, but alas it is only
nature's beauty. The rivulet continues on to the east and south.
name: Rushing Rivulet

desc:
Glistening grey rocks seem to line the ground under this shallow stream. Water,
barely two feet deep, does nothing to diminish the shimmering tone. A few rocks
that have been tossed out of the stream by whatever means, show these stones to
me no more than ordinary. Water spilled outside the riverbed does not glisten
with the same brilliance. Only together do these two elements form such a
beautiful silvery illusion.
name: Rushing Rivulet

desc:
The stream makes a turn to the north here. While the change in direction is
subtle, it is enough to kick up some of the silvery rocks that line the
riverbed. They lie like fish out of water, craving the rare splashes of water
that might keep them from drying out and losing their luminescence.
name: Rushing Rivulet

desc:
High above the much used trail to the east, this is a good place to survey the
surrounding lands. Towards the eastern edge the land is badly eroded and jagged
edges have formed from pieces of this ground falling down the cliff to the trail
below. An adventurous traveller could travel down the same way but returning to
this surface would be impossible.
name: Perilous Mountain Precipice

desc:
Shadowed in the afternoons by the tall mountains in the west, this high place
itself shadows the narrow trail below to the east. The ground is smooth, having
been worn away by wind. The eastern edge is frayed from wind shear and pieces of
this precipice have fallen to the ground below.
name: Perilous Mountain Precipice

desc:
Within sight of the etched trail that runs to the east and south, this area is a
stark contrast to the grasslands on the trail's opposing side. The ground is
uneven and rocky, having collected the droppings of rock, dirt and other debris
from the mountains above.
name: Dusty Foothills

desc:
Tufts of black fur are stuck to low portions of the trees. The coarse black fur
seems to have been left by creatures merely wanting to scratch their backs
against the rough bark. The tree-lined mountaintops stretch on to the north and
south.
name: Majestic Mountaintop

desc:
Rocks, dirt and hay have collected to form this ground, either blown off the
mountain peaks or across the trail to the south. Some rocks have been pressed
deep into the soil from the speed at which they fell from high cliffs.
name: Dusty Foothills

desc:
While still touching the lower clouds, this mountain is far lower than the three
to the west. It looks almost like a child in comparison to them. Not nearly as
impressive, it does boast a burgeoning plant life. A few areas of clover provide
a soft green cover to the ground. Some wild daisies grow adding their white and
gold brilliance to the scene.
name: Cloudcapt Mountains

desc:
These foothills run from farther north and continue southwest. They appear to be
the last resting spot for travellers from the east. A few shrubs are the only
plant life, a stark contrast to the fertile fields to the east. The sunlight
disappears right after noon, when the sun shifts to the western horizon and is
blocked by the towering mountainside.
name: Rising Steeps

desc:
Rocks that have fallen from high peaks and the imposing shadow of the mountains,
seek to dissuade travellers from continuing westward. Deep cuts into the soft
dirt between the rocks show evidence that this land is passable. A few small
plants, barely more than seedlings with a few days growth, are the only
vegetation.
name: Rising Steeps

desc:
From here, the base of the mountains, it is impossible to see at the height to
which these mountains rise. When looking up and to the west, the colossal mounds
block even the sky. In the afternoon, no sunlight touches these foothills. Only
a few bushes, adapted to the shade, manage to grow.
name: Rising Steeps

desc:
In the shadows of the mountains, these foothills see little unfiltered sunlight.
The ground is strewn with rocks and dirt that the wind has swept from the high
peaks. A few stray grasses and shrubs have managed to grow here despite the
inconsistent terrain.
name: Rising Steeps

desc:
High in the clouds, the air surrounding this mountain peak is thin but not
clear. The clouds, that hang about this summit, create a light fog. If not for
the fog, from this high place one could see the entire mountain range and
perhaps even into the meadows and prairie lands below. Breaking through the
misty haze are the tops of trees that disappear once more when covered by higher
clouds. This appears to be one of several peaks atop a large mountain base.
name: Cloudcapt Mountains

desc:
Teeming with life, this hidden basin seems to be a well kept secret from those
who might want to clear away the beautiful wild flowers and grasses to plant
crops. The fertile land lies practically untouched. Only a few wanderers through
the mountains might stumble upon this place accidentally.
name: Sheltered Basin

desc:
Flowers of violet and crimson fill this part of the basin. Their vibrant colour
dominates over the pale green grasses. Some birch trees stand tall in groups of
two or three. The white colour of their peeling bark accents the light airy feel
of this plentiful place. Several berry bushes add to the scene and provide food
for some native animals.
name: Sheltered Basin

desc:
The dark brown dirt on the ground is mixed with light grey ashes giving it a
speckled look. Some unusual small plants grow barely a boot tip high off the
ground. Their tiny leaves almost resemble clover and they cover the ground much
in the same way. Some of the ash has separated from the dirt and lies upon the
plant cover.
name: Ashen Vale

desc:
A painter would be hard pressed to capture the perfect blend of blue and green
that makes up the colour of this thick grass. The shade is one that would have
been saved only for use on portraits commissioned by great lords of the land. It
covers the jagged cliffs here in defiance of any caste system showing its beauty
to all that pass through this place.
name: Regal Cliffs

desc:
Between the slopes of two of the taller western mountain peaks, this pass does
little else but provide a resting place for those weary of climbing. At first
glance it often appears to weave through the mountains providing an easier route
than to climb. However, following the pass westward only leads to a steep
mountainside that must be climbed.
name: Unavailing Mountain Pass

desc:
Tintalle the Kindler, the creator of the stars, best known as Elbereth
Gilthoniel, might consider this mountain a good place to pay tribute to her
greatness. Each night, from atop this celestial peak one can gaze at the
splendour of her creations. The calm night air at this lofty height allows
humbled stargazers the freedom to meditate on the tales told by the
constellations.
name: Celestial Summit

desc:
This narrow ledge is kinder on travellers than other parts of the mountainside,
since the slope is not as steep. Unfortunately, this ridge meanders so much that
it is difficult to tell how much distance, if any, is gained until reaching the
end of the ledge. From then on the climb is arduous at best. To the east, the
land slopes downward through heavy brush.
name: Tapered Mountain Ridge

desc:
The little beck begins to grow fairly rapidly as it flows eastward through the
vale, gathering to itself numerous tiny runnels of water as it flows on
downward. But, although it is confined to a narrow and rocky channel, it still
remains very shallow, and many of the larger boulders sit clear above its
sparkling and foaming surface.
name: A Growing Stream

desc:
Not far to the west the canyon walls draw so close together as to appear to
meet, though the stream below you does proceed just a little further in that
direction. The ground slopes upward both north and west, making it difficult to
walk along the valley. Southward it falls rapidly down to the stream. Only a few
tiny mountain plants can cling to life here, their roots seeking tiny fissures
in the rocky slope.
name: Near the Head of the Valley

desc:
So steep are the walls of this narrow vale that there is virtually no flat
ground upon which to travel. The cliffs loom high above you, both to the north
and to the south, beyond a modest brook. Westward they draw ever closer
together, as the valley climbs into the mountain heights. Little grows upon this
sloping ground, since no top-soil remains to support plant life.
name: In a Steep-Walled Valley

desc:
To the north the valley opens out somewhat, but to the south the creek is
flanked by steep cliffs, which rise high above it. This, however provides an
extra source of waters for the stream, which is widening with every mile it
travels from the western mountains. Though it remains quite shallow, the steep
grade of the valley floor causes it to flow along quite briskly, and as it
buffets protruding rocks and boulders, its surface is covered by white caps.
name: On a Babbling Mountain Stream

desc:
Thick bushes and wild weeds that have grown as large as shrubs line this part of
the narrow ridge. The large plants reduce the space in which travellers may pass
through, making the abrupt ridge seem even more narrow.
name: Tapered Mountain Ridge

desc:
The rough terrain shows signs of its age. The wind has worn away much of the top
soil and lashed at the mountainside. Some grasses grow scattered in the shelter
of large boulders. A few flowering weeds add a bit of colour to this otherwise
two-tone setting of dusty rock and weak green grass.
name: Coarse Mountainside

desc:
Underneath the jagged cliffs lies this hidden valley. It sits perhaps even lower
in elevation than the meadowlands on the eastern side of this mountain range.
Several apple trees grow here providing nourishment for some small animals. Some
bushes laden with unrecognisable purple berries complement the low trees and the
thin green grass.
name: Hidden Hollow

desc:
Thick blades of blue-green grass cover these cliffs like the thick fur of a
bear. Each strand of grass is resilient to the wind and the pressures that may
fall upon them. So thick is the grass, even with the rise and fall of the cliffs
almost no bare soil can be seen. The deep rich colouring of these grasses gives
this whole area a luxurious look.
name: Regal Cliffs

desc:
The uneven ground is covered in wild blue-green grasses. The thick carpet is
undisturbed by the rises and falls of the mountainous terrain. A sharp drop to
the east descends to the lowlands. To the south the soft grass cover continues.
Toward the east the constantly changing slopes of this land seem to reach a
plateau.
name: Regal Cliffs

desc:
This sky-scraping peak, together with the one to the north and the one to the
east, is the most recognisable facet of this mountain range. For miles to the
east, the three peaks dominate the western horizon. The three towering behemoths
point far into the clouds. Only on rare clear days is it possible to spot which
is the tallest of these peaks that seem to share a common mountain base.
name: Cloudcapt Mountains

desc:
About half as high as the mountains to the east, this plateau looks down upon a
canyon to the north and faces steep cliffs to the west. Blades of grass attached
by thin vine-like roots spread for miles. Some saplings that pushed through the
tangle of grass roots have grown into fine young trees. None of the trees are
tall enough to provide any shade.
name: Highland Dale

desc:
This odd shaped canyon connects the low land to the north with the high plateau
to the south. Linear cuts in the canyon walls allow travellers to walk down into
or up out of this place fairly easily. The ground and walls are made of light
coloured solid rock that look as if they could support some weight.
name: Angular Canyon

desc:
In the spring, the scent of strawberries fills the air. They grow wild in
clusters of ten to twelve plants, attracting many small animals hungry for the
sweet fruit. The tall mountain cliffs shadow this place, making it an ideal
picnic spot for rodents and travellers alike. Looking up from here one can only
see about half the way up the mountainside.
name: Hidden Hollow

desc:
Descending more gently than its neighbouring slopes this terrain increases in
greenery as it leads southward. Scant grasses grow thicker in that direction
along with some berry laden bushes. The lands to the east and west are far more
barren.
name: Coarse Mountainside

desc:
This portion of mountainside resembles giant steps made from mud. Some of these
natural steps are as tall as an elf, others are the height of a hobbit. They
span widths between five and fifty feet between drops. The vertical portions are
host to a variety of wild grasses and some hardy bushes.
name: Ascending Cliffs

desc:
Hugging a steep cliff-face to the south, the brook makes its way along its rocky
channel, located at the bottom of a long valley. Running from west to east, the
gorge loses altitude steadily, and so the burn flows along rapidly. Its lively
waters send up a constant fine spray, which hangs in the air like a very fine
mist.
name: The Fast-Flowing Waters of Nimrodel

desc:
The lie of the land is quite steep, as the valley climbs into the arms of the
westerly mountains. Sloping also to the north, the ground climbs toward the base
of a mighty cliff face, which rears vertically, far above your head. Not far
below you, to the south, a small stream makes its way from the west to the east,
flowing out of the heights.
name: A Rising Valley

desc:
A gaping tunnel leads into the side of the mountain to the north, whilst to the
south all that faces you is the edge of the cliffs, with a sheer drop-off to the
valley below. This fairly narrow ledge slopes gently upward to the west, whilst
to the east it ends abruptly.
name: A Mountainside

desc:
This is a tunnel which runs underneath the mountain, it is very dark and you can
see lithe shadows dancing on the wall. The tunnel spreads north, and to the
south you can exit the mountain. Above you is a huge crack which leads back
outside.
name: Inside the Mountain

desc:
This is a tunnel which runs underneath the mountain. It is very dark and you can
see lithe shadows dancing on the wall. The tunnel leads into a large empty space
to the east, and to the south you can follow it into the depths of the mountain.
name: Inside the Mountain

desc:
The trail continues onwards stretching from east to west. There is nothing much
to see except the small stones and pebbles that line the floor. Your vision is
completely blocked by the rock walls that surround the trail. A crisp and fresh
scent is carried on the air by the mountain wind.
name: A Strange Trail

desc:
A trail winds its way through the mountain enclosure, hidden from all external
view. One can only wonder how it came to be. The space is narrow and the
rockfaces stretch high above your head. The wind is whistling a strange and
eerie tune, probably due to the way it blows through this rock formation.
name: A Strange Trail

desc:
You move on the little path which has broken away from the main trail. The path
promptly comes to an end at your feet. Your options are to continue west, back
along the path, or to crawl through the narrow crack which leads downwards to an
empty darkness.
name: Off the Path

desc:
You are on a fairly narrow ledge, high up on a mountain side. A few small
patches of thin grass grow here, as well as a few tiny mountain flowers, but
there seems to be no other sign of life nearby. Higher up, and to the west,
stands a large plateau.
name: A Mountainside

desc:
During spring's thaw, snow turns into small rills, cascading down the mountains
north and south of here forming this fast flowing rivulet. Natural springs
welling up from the ground give life to this swift moving waterway throughout
the rest of the year. The cool clear water amplifies the brilliance of the
silver stones that line this stream's bed. The stones, which once had jagged
edges, are now worn smooth by the steady, even flow of the water. Over time,
these rocks will be nothing but pebbles in the bottom of the stream.
name: Rushing Rivulet

desc:
One might expect this mountain's peak to point to the heavens, if it had a
pointed peak. Instead what is here is ground so flat, it is as if the mountain's
summit was knocked off with a hammer and chisel. The level ground is covered in
wild weeds and a few young trees that someday will give this apex its pointed
top.
name: Chiselled Apex

desc:
Plants cover this portion of the mountainside. Large bushes keep the soil from
sliding down the slope. Wild flowers grow in clusters farther down the mountain
to the south and west.
name: Rugged Headlands

desc:
These cliffs are rather devoid of plant life. The odd shrub grows in the arid
soil. A few grassy patches grow on bits of level ground. The land here is too
dry and rocky to support much else. Deposits of dirt and mud run down the slope
of the mountainside pile here sometimes two to three feet high.
name: Ascending Cliffs

desc:
The waters of the stream swirl and roil here, as they gather in a small pool at
the edge of an almost vertical drop-off. For whilst the lands to the north make
a fairly steady ascent from east to west, the channel through which the brook
makes its way is stepped here. At the rim of the pool, the water flows over a
flat slab of stone, then tumbles down to another pool below.
name: At the Top of a Waterfall

desc:
Hemmed in to both north and south by tall mountain ridges which draw closer
toward one another as they venture westward, the valley grows more narrow as it
climbs in that direction. At its lowest point, not far to the south of here, a
small stream races along the rocky floor.
name: In a Narrowing Valley

desc:
A ceiling of solid rock hangs heavily, not too far above your head. Beneath your
feet the stony ground has been worn smooth in places, and the cold, stone walls
bear signs of tooling. It is impossible to tell what type of design was once
etched there, and as to whose hand did this work, you can only guess.
name: Under a Rocky Overhang

desc:
The cliff face turns east and south here, and a fairly spacious rocky ledge
protrudes from this angle. The surface of the stone is extremely uneven and is
pitted with tiny cracks and fissures. Sloping off quite rapidly in both
directions, it seems hardly to be a safe place to linger.
name: Atop a Rocky Ledge

desc:
You are within a massive chamber of such cavernous dimensions that it might
easily hold more than two hundred men. Its immense proportions mean that it is
almost impossible to see one end from the other, but careful exploration shows
that there are but three exits from this enormous space. To the west is a wide
tunnel through which cool, fresh air flows. Set in the floor to the south is
access to a lower series of tunnels, and in the eastern wall is a narrow
opening, which appears to lead upwards.
name: A Dark Cavern

desc:
The trail continues onwards stretching from east to west. There is not much to
see except the small stones and pebbles that line the rocky floor. Your vision
is completely blocked by the rock walls that surround the trail. A crisp and
fresh scent is carried on the air by the mountain wind.
name: Following a Strange Trail

desc:
Here the trail bends, and you can follow it either northwards or westwards. The
surroundings of the trail remain pretty much the same, desolate, empty and
bland, with no signs of animal life or vegetation.
name: Along a Strange Trail

desc:
Climbing steeply from the cavern to the west, this narrow, but high-roofed
tunnel meanders up through the interior of the mountain. In places it appears as
if the floor has been carved into very crude stairs, but it is hard to tell if
these are truly artifacts, or if some trick of nature has accidentally made the
passage look this way. Whichever is the case, they are an invaluable aid in
traversing this precipitous shaft.
name: A Steep and Winding Passage

desc:
Hugging the face of the cliff, the land here has climbed more rapidly than has
the valley floor below, creating a rocky shelf which is several feet wide, and
at some distance above the surrounding countryside. In places the ground has
been worn quite smooth, but rocks and stones are scattered all around. Some of
these are fairly large, and it seems that perhaps they are not the result of
natural landslides, for many appear to be arranged in rows, as if forming the
foundation of some dwelling, long since ruined.
name: Above the Floor of the Valley

desc:
The valley here is somewhat stepped, with three distinct levels. Above you, to
the north, a ledge nestles against the face of a tall cliff. To the south, and
below the level of the stream bank lies a dark pool, fed by a merry little
waterfall. Climbing to the west, the sloping floor of the valley draws level
with the upper reaches of the fast-flowing mountain stream.
name: Climbing a Mountain Vale

desc:
A deep pool stands at the bottom of the waterfall. Although the drop is not
especially high, the falls nevertheless send up a constant light spray of cool
water. As she flows on eastward from here, following a channel along the base of
the cliffs to the south, Nimrodel is forced into a narrow gully, which causes
some of the water to swirl backward, making the surface here quite choppy for
such a small stream.
name: A Pool beneath the Waterfall

desc:
Trees line the eastern side of one of the great mountains in this range. They
grow out of soil at a sharp angle, often with their roots exposed. Only plants
with roots that dig in deep could even hope to survive in this area's loose
soil. The few shrubs and wild plants that give the mountainside a bit of green
cover grow densely along a few flat ledges along the slope. Northward, a
waterfall can be seen in the distance.
name: Rugged Headlands

desc:
This rocky precipice gives travellers a unique vantage point from which to
witness the etched trail below to the north and gaze over the fertile plains
down toward the east. Looking straight down in either directions rocks fallen
from this point can be seen littering the ground.
name: A Jagged Escarpment

desc:
The ground to the western edge of this plateau seems to be crumbling away with
the wear of the wind. Below the weathered edge rocks fall and litter the narrow
trail below. It seems possible to traverse the cliff and reach the trail below.
Returning to this point the same way, however, would be impossible.
name: Top of an Escarpment

desc:
Aglow in the sunlight or moonlight, these tall grasses carry a deep orange
pigment. Their sturdy stalks grow strong, thriving in these rolling lands.
Oftentimes they grow so thick it cannot be seen where the hills slope down and
then rise up again. Animals can easily conceal their form in this thick, grassy
cover.
name: Auburn Hills

desc:
The smell of clover fills the air. The thick carpet of these leafy plants
stretches for at least a mile. The leisurely traveller might be tempted to rest
and look for one clover with four leaves. The plant's horizontal growth patterns
cause it to spread far in every direction rather than skyward like the fields of
flaxen wheat grasses to the east.
name: Edge of an Open Meadow

desc:
Greener than surrounding meadowlands, this area is a haven for birds that nibble
and pick at the soil in search of a worm or grub. Some young trees are just
strong enough to support the feathered creatures' weight should they desire a
high perch on which to plan their search. On the higher branches is the start of
a nest that one such bird is building in hope that the fertile land will provide
plenty of food for a coming family.
name: Meadowlands

desc:
Blades of intertwined green and gold grasses grow so closely together they hide
the dark soil beneath them. Small hills separated by only a few feet between
each other create the look of flaxen waves rolling toward a far off shore. Gusts
of wind have spread these grasses' seed far to the north and west.
name: Golden Meadow

desc:
Grasses the colour of sunshine stretch out in every direction. A few scattered
trees grow here, their thick dark trunks providing some shade when the sun
shines from the east. From one tree hangs a small beehive which sends the
lingering scent of honey drifting on the wind.
name: Deep within the Meadowlands

desc:
Honey coloured grasses mixing with verdant shrubs create a patchwork of green
and gold. In the spring, the shrubs are covered in ruby coloured berries that
are quickly devoured by small herbivores. In the fall what was vibrant plant
life dries up becoming thick clumps of hay, enough to make many bales should a
farmer need.
name: Golden Meadow

desc:
Light coloured wheat grasses grow beside the carved trail to the west. However,
those blades of grass closest to the trail have been so often trampled upon by
careless travellers they have lost some of their hardiness. To the east the
grasses grow thicker, stronger and in some places even waist-high.
name: Edge of the Golden Meadow

desc:
The trampled grasses are apparently the result of travellers straying off the
trail to the northwest. The natural design of this golden meadow has been marred
with hoof marks, abandoned campsites and the remains of butchered game. Long
blades of what would be tall grasses, have been bent in half and hang sadly
beyond repair.
name: Corner of the Golden Meadow

desc:
A few travellers, straying from the trail to the north, have damaged the golden
grasses that cover this land. The tall stalks have been bent as they were pushed
aside or trampled to make passage easier. The few rocks that at one time dotted
this area are often found gathered together where someone has made camp.
name: Bordering the Golden Meadow

desc:
Wheat grasses the colour of cornsilk rise into the air knee-high. The scampering
of a small animal creates a domino effect of bending stalks that make this
meadow glisten in the afternoon sun. At night cool winds blow the winsome blades
back and forth, each blade rebounding back into place.
name: Golden Meadow

desc:
The soil here must be rich in nutrients as there are very few spots of ground
where the wild grasses do not grow. The dirt is a deep brown and would prove
resistant to winds that might carry away more arid soil. The prints of some
animals are visible in the dirt but then are obscured where the grasses grow. To
the north and west, the meadowland ends abruptly along the edges of a trail.
name: Edge of the Meadow

desc:
The dark bark of this group of old trees is covered in moss. Its moist, green
colour reflects both the sun's and the moon's light making the trees look like
carved jade statues. The trees' leaves, almost the same colour as the moss, add
to the statuesque illusion. The wet feel of the moss however shatters the image
and reveals this forest to be very much alive.
name: Jade Grove

desc:
Amber stalks of wild wheat grasses fill these hills giving off a sweet wholesome
smell. The tall blades of grass grow skyward two to three feet above the soil.
Unlike the wheat planted by careful farmers, no harvester has cut down or
controlled these grains since they took seed. The only danger to their continued
growth comes in the form of strong gusts of wind.
name: Auburn Hills

desc:
A spot of exposed dirt provides a sharp contrast to the rest of these rolling
hills. On this one low hill, the topsoil has been blown away and all that
remains is a reddish dirt, so thick it could be mistaken for clay. A middle-aged
tree grows here on an angle. Its roots have been exposed to the elements as they
protrude from the ground. All around, the hills are covered in grains the colour
of the sun on a blistering hot day.
name: Auburn Hills

desc:
A soft amber glow emanates from the winsome grains that grow here. These sun
kissed hills rise and fall like tides. The small hills and shallow gullies
covered with tall wild grasses provide excellent cover for small animals as they
scurry to collect seeds or hunt animals even smaller than themselves.
name: Auburn Hills

desc:
This uneven land rises and falls every few feet yet the tips of the tall golden
grasses remain even. Occasionally a gust of strong wind blows the grasses flat
and the inconsistencies of the terrain are more visible. In places the shifting
height of the dirt exposes the roots of the few trees that have grown to
maturity. Holes, a few inches wide, have been dug into the ground.
name: Golden Prairie

desc:
The golden grasses grow thick in this area, cushioning the footsteps of
travellers to this land. Tiny insects, almost too small to see, feed on the
grains. A few scattered trees dot the landscape but they provide meagre to no
shade during the day. To the east the land continues on flat and fairly
featureless.
name: Golden Prairie

desc:
The sunlight, that reflects off this land during the daylight hours, has
nurtured these golden grasses. They thrive and form a thick carpet over the
soil. The lay of the land is flat and there are few trees to block the sun's
rays. When the wind blows the blades of wheat grasses shimmer like gold. The
soil is arid yet rich with minerals.
name: Golden Prairie

desc:
The trees here are tall and thick, the lower parts of their trunks covered with
moss and fungi. Smells of moisture and rot are in the air. Among the animal
tracks on the ground one can see more regular tracks which look as if they had
been left by humanoid creatures.
name: Dense Forest

desc:
The tall trees of this forest loom high over the shorter ones which look as if
they gave up trying to grow high enough to reach the sunlight. Smells of moss
and fungi linger in the damp air. Small insects feed upon the moss. Some animal
tracks can be seen on the ground.
name: Deep Forest

desc:
The trees here stand somewhat apart and the sky is visible in the gaps between
their branches. Many shrubs and bushes grow on the ground beneath the trees.
Grass and a few bright forest flowers adorn the area and to the north the trees
grow taller as the forest becomes more dense.
name: Light Forest

desc:
Green grass grows plentifully on the hillsides along with many varieties of
flowers. Many bushes, some that produce fruit and some that don't, grow all
around together with an occasional stunted tree. A little to the north one can
see the beginnings of a forest. A path of trampled grass crosses the terrain
from the north to the south; it seems that many feet have walked this way.
name: Rolling Hills

desc:
In all directions the rolling hills continue. Covering the ground is a mixture
of different grasses, some of them brown, others green and gold. Large bushes
with black fruit on them grow together in thick knots. Some of these bushes also
have sharp thorns on them that could easily penetrate the bare skin of a
careless traveller. A path of trampled grass runs north-south across the
terrain; the path is little more than a few tracks running side by side.
name: Rolling Hills

desc:
The light golden grass that covers the slopes of the hills is gently caressed by
the wind that carries itself through the landscape. Trees, bushes and occasional
flowers enliven the even hillsides and the hilltops. All the trees have one
distinct feature: they are stunted, as if for some reason they were unable to
reach their full height. Jutting out of a grassy hillside, a large boulder
attracts the eye; the few tracks that lead through the terrain seem to converge
at the boulder.
name: Rolling Hills

desc:
The landscape here is ever-changing, as with every gust of wind the movement of
the waves of grass makes small hills and deep ravines appear seemingly out of
nowhere. Huge clumps of dry grass are carried across the plains by the wind. A
few small, wiry-looking bushes that grow here and there appear to be the only
static part of the picture.
name: Golden Plains

desc:
This area is slightly different from the plains all around. More trees grow
here, and they are somewhat larger than those in the surrounding plains, and
some of them even bear fruit. Occasional clumps of bushes also dot the even
landscape.
name: Light Plains

desc:
These plains continue to the east and north. The gentle weave of the grass in
the wind ripples like waves in the sea. Gently rolling hills carry onwards over
the entire plain. Many varieties of bushes, flowers and trees can be seen
everywhere.
name: Vast Plains

desc:
The ground here is grey and unpopulated, except for some thorny bushes and
withered brown grass. These seemingly dead fields stretch out in all directions,
making it easy to get lost among the similar surroundings. Occasionally, a bird
can be seen flying high above the land, perhaps an indication of something other
than deserted plains nearby.
name: Dry Plains

desc:
As the land slopes down from the west to the east, the grass grows thinner and
thinner as the plains become more barren. The grass that does grow here is
mostly brownish in colour and looks wilted, more dead than alive. The trees that
do grow here are low and stunted.
name: Sparse Plains

desc:
Light green grass waves back and forth in the gentle, yet persistent, wind. A
few stunted trees grow here, their brownish-coloured leaves hanging limply from
their thin branches. A few bushes grow by the side of the trees, some of them
bearing pink flowers and orange fruit.
name: Light Plains

desc:
The consistency of the look of these plains could be amazing to a traveller used
to faster changes in the terrain. As far as the eye can see, green and light
golden grass covers the earth, its even expanse only rarely interrupted by a
half-grown tree or a clump of bushes.
name: Light Plains

desc:
The rolling landscape seems hardly to change, as the grass, green and light
golden, is everywhere, but to the south you can see a small copse of pine-trees
standing in a circle. While it is many miles to the nearest forest, some of the
pines appear so ancient that they they might well be the survivors from the time
when the forest of Lorien extended much farther south. Inside the circle of
trees pine-needles cover the grass like a thick carpet.
name: Light Plains

desc:
The ground is covered in thick grass the blades of which are light golden in
colour. The grass sways ever so gently in the slow, caressing wind. Animal
tracks are easy to spot as the grass appears to have leaned aside to allow them
passage.
name: Light Plains

desc:
The ground here is moist, made fertile by the frequent rain and abundant
sunshine. The grass grows high and thick, and its blades are mostly light golden
in colour, in some spots tinged with green or brown. Small insects can be seen
feeding upon the bountiful grass.
name: Light Plains

desc:
The surrounding area is one teeming with wildlife and vegetation. Thick and
bountiful bushes, which during the spring and summer produce many kinds of
fruit, grow everywhere. Many trees grow here as well, but most of these are mere
saplings. Every free spot on the ground is covered with grass which grows up to
two feet in height.
name: Light Plains

desc:
Roots of a tree entwine around a huge rock that lies against the side of the
hill that rises to the north. The rock is almost completely covered with roots.
A small spring comes down from the top of the hill and runs down the hillside,
forming a small pond at the bottom. Many animal tracks can be seen in the mud
around the spring, as they come from all over to drink from this water-hole.
name: By a Hillside

desc:
In this area of the plains there are almost no trees, but there are many dry
tree-roots in the ground, apparently remaining from the time when this land was
more forested. Thorny bushes dot the landscape, and some berries can be seen
growing on them. The land slopes upwards gently to the west.
name: Light Plains

desc:
This flat area is open to all the winds and seems an unlikely spot to find a
lonely tree, yet here one grows, casting its shade upon the ground. As far as
eye can see, green plains stretch out in all directions, waves passing through
the sea of grass with every gust of wind.
name: Light Plains

desc:
Winds blow constantly through the level landscape, filling the dry air with
scents and sounds of the surrounding lands. With every gust of wind the thick
green grass bends back and forth, making a gentle rustling sound that seems to
fade as soon as it reaches your ears.
name: Light Plains

desc:
The thick green grass that covers these plains seems to grow even as you watch,
its tall stems concealing every track and every mound or depression in the
ground. A few clusters of bushes and an occasional tree can also be seen in the
plains, rising like islands from the grassy sea.
name: Light Plains

desc:
A well-beaten path has been made here through the jungle of grass. Without it
passage on foot would be much harder if at all possible. All kinds of tracks can
be seen on the path: tracks of various wild animals, hoof-prints of many horses
and occasionally unmistakably humanoid tracks, prints of boots of a traveller
that walked through this area on foot.
name: Light Plains

desc:
The land here is barren and dry. The sparse vegetation consists of grass and
occasional bushes. The grass is brownish and not too lively, but the bushes
appear well adapted to this type of environment, their branches bearing long
thorns and their roots digging deep into the ground.
name: Sparse Plains

desc:
Withered, dying trees can be seen everywhere, wilted by the power of the
abundant sunlight and lack of water. Brown, curled leaves, fallen from the
surrounding trees, litter the ground. Weathered tufts of brownish grass are
spread liberally about the area.
name: Dry Plains

desc:
A light breeze picks its way through the forest, slightly stirring the leaves on
the branches and the ground. Pine and oak trees are easy to spot amongst the
golden crowns and slender branches of the mellyrn. Moss completely covers the
base of some of the trees. Small insects can be seen feeding off the moss.
name: Mixed Forest

desc:
Mellyrn and birches mingle here, the white boles of the birches mixing with the
silvery-grey boles of the golden trees. Some fallen leaves can be seen on the
ground, but they bear no sign of rot or decay. Except for the singing of birds
and the rustling of dry leaves, the forest is quiet, the great mellyrn standing
solemn and motionless like guardians keeping watch.
name: Mixed Forest

desc:
Ancient trees, mellyrn and birches, grow here side by side with small saplings.
The green and golden tree-crowns merge, almost completely hiding the sky which
is visible only when the wind moves the branches apart. Though the forest
appears peaceful, there is a feeling of watchfulness, as if some unseen eyes
were looking down from the tree-tops.
name: Mixed Forest

desc:
A few mellyrn brighten the forest here with their golden crowns and silvery
boles, but the rest of the trees are mostly dark pines which seem even darker by
comparison. The ground below the trees is cluttered by fallen branches, leaves
and pine-needles in which small insects can be seen scurrying. Fungi and moss
are plentiful here, as the soil is moist and not much sunlight reaches the
ground.
name: Mixed Forest

desc:
Little outside light reaches the forest ground where darkened shadows fool the
eye into seeing things. Leaves of the bushes rustle quietly as some unseen
animal stalks through the forest. The trees themselves are enormous, their great
trunks rising into the roof of leaves and disappearing out of sight. An
occasional cry of a bird can be heard from above, but mostly the forest is
quiet.
name: Mixed Forest

desc:
Trees of all kinds grow here, clustered all together. Pine, oak, and maple
thrive in this forest. Pine-needles, leaves, and branches cover the ground but
in some places small saplings pop through the cover as they fight for survival.
Thick bushes and other undergrowth cover the ground.
name: Mixed Forest

desc:
This part of the forest is so quiet that the light rustling of the tree-leaves
in the wind can be heard distinctly. The forest consists mostly of elms, but
here and there you can spot an occasional clump of the golden mellyrn. There is
little undergrowth and the ground under the trees is mostly bare, covered only
by a carpet of fallen leaves.
name: Dense Forest

desc:
Trees grow densely here amidst the other forms of vegetation. Some red and blue
forest flowers grow on the ground among shrubs and bushes. Green moss covers the
trunks of some trees, and small fungi grow around the base of the others.
name: Dense Forest

desc:
A few trees that once stood proud and tall now lie here, fallen to the earth.
This has created an opportunity for small saplings and other vegetation to rise
where they once stood. The ground below the trees is covered by dead leaves and
fallen branches which shelter many small forest insects.
name: Deep Forest

desc:
The forest is dense, and the trees are thick and tall. Many different species
grow together, broad-leaf trees mixing with the conifers. The wind gently
caresses the leaves, making them stir slightly, and their whisper carries
through the forest.
name: Deep Forest

desc:
Mellyrn can be spotted easily among the other trees as they stand out with their
silvery boles and golden crowns. Fallen leaves lie on the ground, mixed with an
occasional golden blossom; the leaves are quite dry and crumble whenever anyone
steps upon them. As the wind gently stirs the tree-branches, glimpses of the sky
can be seen. Calls of birds and various animal noises echo loudly from the
depths of the forest.
name: Dense Forest

desc:
A soft wind scatters the leaves that have fallen on the ground and gently sways
the leaves on the tree-branches. Small insects scurry about as their cover is
blown away. A few fallen trees lie here, creating a perfect environment for many
different kinds of fungi to grow.
name: Dense Forest

desc:
Fallen leaves and branches cover the moist, rich earth in which many kinds of
bushes and shrubs and a few tree-saplings thrive, reaching to the light. Animal
tracks can be seen among the undergrowth, a few of them apparently made by
rather large animals.
name: Dense Forest

desc:
A few bird nests can be seen in the tree-branches, half-hidden by the thick
canopy. The moist, soft soil is covered by fallen leaves and is apparently a
perfect place for many plants to grow. Short bushes and various forest flowers
are quite common here and seem to grow everywhere, the bright colours of their
blooms enlivening the forest.
name: Deep Forest

desc:
A few small rocks stick out of the ground here, half-hidden by the vegetation.
Moss and fungi grow all around, covering the bark of many trees and the surface
of the rocks. A few mushrooms can be seen on the forest floor, peeking out from
under the fallen leaves.
name: Deep Forest

desc:
The trees grow very closely together here, forming a dense canopy overhead. This
canopy partially blocks out the sky, while making a perfect nesting spot for
many birds. Roots of some trees have popped out from under the earth, making
travel difficult.
name: Deep Forest

desc:
Horse stables take up the greater part of this barn. There is a smell of hay and
horse manure in the air. Many farmer utensils including pitchforks and shovels
are stacked up against the northern wall. A ramp is against one wall, leading up
to the roof of this building. A lot of campfire ashes can be seen on the ground,
apparently left here by many people seeking shelter from the forces of nature.
name: Inside the Barn

desc:
Short bushes and small trees hide a small path that leads to a wooden barn in
the west. The forest looks as if it had been thinned by loggers as there are
many dead trees and decaying stumps. There is a lot of undergrowth here since
the trees are not so thick, and the sunlight is able to reach the ground. Some
of the grass looks as if it had been grazed.
name: Light Forest

desc:
The thick trees rise up hundreds of feet. Little shrubs or bushes grow here, as
very little sunlight reaches the ground, making it hard for them to survive. In
the spot where an old tree had fallen, creating an opening in the canopy, many
young trees fight over a place under the opening that would allow them to catch
some sunlight.
name: Deep Forest

desc:
The thick green canopy formed by the foliage of the tall trees blocks out the
life-giving sun. Thus most vegetation that usually grows in forests seems to
avoid this place. There is little grass, bushes and flowers. Instead the ground
is covered by fallen leaves and branches which slowly decay away.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
This part of the forest is quite dark, and there is little vegetation besides
trees. A few bushes grow by the side of the tree-trunks, but their branches seem
too thin and their leaves appear too pale. The trees are the dominating force
here, growing so close together that their foliage forms an uninterrupted canopy
which blocks out the sky. Occasional bird calls echo down from the
tree-branches.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
Trees here grow slightly apart, and the ground below them is covered by grass.
Most of the trees aren't nearly as tall as those one can see to the north. To
the south endless plains stretch as far as the eye can see. The soft green grass
seems to be everywhere, covering every inch of the ground.
name: Light Forest

desc:
The forest ends to the south as the land opens into a wide grassy plain. Nearing
the edge of the forest, the trees appear to decrease in size as well as in
number. Occasional animal sounds can be heard from the north where the trees
stand closer and the undergrowth is more dense. Many small bushes grow here,
some of them bearing small berries.
name: Light Forest

desc:
Many different kinds of trees grow here, together with other kinds of
vegetation. Bushes, flowers, and many different kinds of grass are everywhere.
The colourful flowers form a dense carpet, surrounded by thick grass. In some
spots the grass has been trampled by many animals and is turning brown.
name: Light Forest

desc:
A light wind gently moves the grass as it makes its way through the plains.
Clusters of various flowers and bushes are scattered all around. Some of the
bushes have black fruit growing on them. An occasional tree can be seen, but
they aren't clustered together like in a forest. The forest of Lorien can be
seen in the north, but passage in that direction is blocked by a wall of a
wooden building.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
To the south the trees start to give way to grass and other undergrowth, mostly
flowers and bushes, but here the forest is quite dense. The tree-branches block
out parts of the sky, but in places large patches of the sky can be seen. Fallen
leaves blanket the thin layer of grass that grows on the ground. Thick knots of
bushes create barriers in some spots, making passage difficult.
name: Deep Forest

desc:
Leaves and fallen tree-trunks cover the ground. Here and there rocks peep out
from underneath the cover. The rocks are covered with green moss and small
insects that feed on this moss. Large trees grow everywhere, making it
impossible to see the sky, as their canopies combine to form together one
gigantic canopy.
name: Deep Forest

desc:
The ground here is completely covered by debris, mostly fallen leaves and
branches. The trees that grow here are of many different varieties and sizes,
yet they all grow mixed together. In a spot where the sunlight can shine down
through the canopy, a circle of blue flowers grows amid the grass.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
The forest here consists mostly of birches, ashes and rowans and is quite light
and airy. The ground is covered with green grass on which many dry tree-leaves
are scattered, rustling with every movement of the air. To the south the trees
recede as the forest opens up into a wide grassy plain.
name: Light Forest

desc:
Light golden grass grows wildly here. Many bushes grow together, and on a few of
them one can see some kind of fruit. Flowers of all varieties and colours, blue,
red, orange, white and purple, dot the landscape. The plains extend south as far
as the eye can see.
name: Golden Plains

desc:
Light golden grass covers the area, waving back and forth from the lightest
breeze. Underneath the grass lies fertile earth that sits there untouched by a
hand of a farmer. Traces left by many animals can be seen clearly by the way the
grass has been bent and torn.
name: Golden Plains

desc:
To the south the plains stretch out seemingly to the very horizon. The land is
populated by many animals, as indicated by the many small burrows that can be
found here. The fertile earth is suitable for almost any kind of vegetation.
Stunted trees intermingle with bushes and flowers of many different colours.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
Many different grasses thrive here, fed by the constant rain and warmed by the
rays of the sun. Swaying gently in the wind that crosses the plains, the tall
grass rustles slightly. Small crickets can be heard constantly chirping.
name: Rolling Hills

desc:
The hillsides and the land between the hills is a mix of grassland and forest. A
lot of trees grow to the north, but the more south you look, the less trees you
see and the more grass. The size of the trees appears to decrease with their
number. To the south the land grows more even and less forested and the wind
picks up speed, as there aren't many trees to interfere with its movement.
name: Rolling Hills

desc:
The forest here is thinner than to the north, as trees have given way to the
undergrowth of thick bushes, shrubs and flowers. As the undergrowth grows more
lush, the trees seem to shrink and grow stunted. Yet farther to the south the
undergrowth disperses as well as the land stretches out into an endless grassy
plain.
name: Edge of the Forest

desc:
To the north the forest grows thicker and darker, but to the south the gaps
between the trees grow wider until eventually the trees disappear and the
terrain opens up into a wide plain that stretches all the way to the horizon.
The forest ground is soft and moist, and the undergrowth consists mostly of
small shrubs and bright forest flowers.
name: Edge of the Forest

desc:
The air here is fresh and clear, allowing to see for a great distance. A forest
seems to start in the north, but looking southward you can see nothing except a
vast expanse of plains and low hills. There is little vegetation except for the
ever-present grass. Trees cling to the hillsides, but for a few copses of tall
pines, they are dwarf-trees, barely distinguishable from bushes.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The light golden grass brightens the plains by day, and even at night is still
easy to see. Bending ever so gently in the wind, it is everywhere. There is no
other vegetation except for a few large bushes that grow here almost to the
height of stunted trees. These bushes all bear some kind of black fruit.
name: Golden Plains

desc:
Rising and falling, the land changes slightly, but everywhere it is covered by
grass. A few stringy bushes with pale-green leaves grow here next to a couple of
bent trees that look more dead than alive. Very rarely one can see an occasional
flower lift its head out of the deep grass.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The grass here is wilted and brown. Few animal tracks can be seen here, but many
small insects can be found hiding underneath rocks that protect them from the
environment. Most of the vegetation has been swept away by the wind along with
much of the topsoil.
name: Dry Plains

desc:
Grass grows bountifully here as the plains continue on. Short grass, tall grass,
golden grass and green grass all mix together to form a thick blanket over the
earth. Occasional trees can be spotted among the plains, some of them growing
alone and some in circles around a few earthen mounds. Small insects jump around
in the grass.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The grass here is quite dry, and with your every movement its slight rustling
makes small insects jump away. There is little traffic through these plains, and
broken and bent grass-stems make any tracks easy to see. The few bushes that can
be seen here are huge and grow together in clumps. The rolling plains continue
in all directions except to the north where the land becomes more hilly.
name: Rolling Plains

desc:
To the north the edge of a forest can be seen, but in all other directions
seemingly endless plains extend as far as the eye can see. Stunted trees grow
along the sides of the few low hills that can be seen among the plains. A deep
carpet of grass covers the ground. Animal tracks have worn a few paths in the
grass, making it easier to travel through the area.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
The grass here is tall, and occasional breezes send it rippling like a wave on
the sea, the smooth passage of the grassy wave unbroken except where it runs
into a small mound. A cluster of bushes grows against the mound, their short
branches bearing dark, almost black, fruit. Every time the wind passes across
the plains, many different kinds of flowers send their seeds out and the wind
gently blows them away.
name: Grassy Plains

desc:
A small copse of young pine trees grows around a small hill that rises above the
thick carpet of grass. The hill is just a few yards in diameter and its sides
are covered with pine-needles and pine-cones. Many pine-cones are broken or
